title: Enhanced Group Communication via Companion Packet Forwarding

messages.author: floeschie
messages.text: I was asked to raise attention to a feature request I've posted on GitHub, which seems to represent a need that has been raised multiple times: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/599
messages.content_html: I was asked to raise attention to a feature request I've posted on GitHub, which seems to represent a need that has been raised multiple times: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/599
embeds_html:
[Feature Request] Enhanced Group Communication via Companion Packet...
Dear MeshCore Developers, I'd like to propose a new feature that would significantly improve group topology and communication in specific scenarios. Current Problem When I'm out in the wood...
messages.reactions_html: 2

messages.author: TKW1/Seagull ZL1JFM
messages.text: Trying to get my head around how this would work in the wild. My use case would be I just started writing a whole use-case and came to the conclusion we could over think this... (edited)

messages.reply_html:
mcarper
There might be a way to use acks. If the companion regularly hears the message and the ack from a repeater then don't re broadcast it. If it doesn't hear the ack then it can rebroadcast as the repeater probably didn't hear the message.

messages.author: floeschie
messages.text: Maybe it is easier to limit this scenario to a room server? Assuming that as a companion I know which other companions are in this room server: As a companion, if I have direct contact to a room server, and if I hear messages for the room server from another companion without ACK from the room server, then relay this messages between the room server and the companion. Stop relaying as soon as the other companion gets ACKs from the room server again.
messages.content_html: Maybe it is easier to limit this scenario to a room server? Assuming that as a companion I know which other companions are in this room server: As a companion, if I have direct contact to a room server, and if I hear messages for the room server from another companion without ACK from the room server, then relay this messages between the room server and the companion. Stop relaying as soon as the other companion gets ACKs from the room server again.

messages.author: floeschie
messages.text: I have no deep knowledge about the technical insights of this idea, just looking from a user's perspective… But it would make sense to me (and fulfill my use case) if this feature would work with Room Server settings only. And as this would only happen in areas with bad coverage, or a low number of repeaters I figure the impact on the mesh is low. (edited)

messages.author: floeschie
messages.text: You mean like the Group Mode feature would not be dependent on a scenario with a room server, but be available to any private Channel (e. g. in the channel settings)? Wouldn't that impact the mesh, if everyone enables the private mode for their channel? I thought limiting this to room server setups would also limit the use case to a very distinct group of people within a limited area. (edited)
messages.content_html: You mean like the Group Mode feature would not be dependent on a scenario with a room server, but be available to any private Channel (e. g. in the channel settings)? Wouldn't that impact the mesh, if everyone enables the private mode for their channel? I thought limiting this to room server setups would also limit the use case to a very distinct group of people within a limited area. (edited)

messages.author: TKW1/Seagull ZL1JFM
messages.text: No, because selecting Group Mode requires a second set of Lora parameters with a different frequency.
messages.content_html: No, because selecting Group Mode requires a second set of Lora parameters with a different frequency.

messages.author: TKW1/Seagull ZL1JFM
messages.text: The more I think about it, this bit seems to fit the bill nicely: "when adding a channel, designate the channel as Group Mode and assign the alternate LoRa settings to it" That way, you could have multiple Group Mode channels with different LoRa settings (edited)

messages.author: VasiliSk
messages.text: So what i got against client rebroadcast: 1. Basically a bunch of devices in pockets etc and with low quality antenna will all hear well and rebroadcast over each other creating noise and collisions vs how it is now and only properly placed repeaters with good antenna do the work and it actually works 2. people hate receiving 1000 nodes from dude on airplane Issue: meshcore does not allow building local mesh networks for hiking and other activities with friends, when you dont have need or/and desire to setup repeater
messages.content_html: So what i got against client rebroadcast: 1. Basically a bunch of devices in pockets etc and with low quality antenna will all hear well and rebroadcast over each other creating noise and collisions vs how it is now and only properly placed repeaters with good antenna do the work and it actually works 2. people hate receiving 1000 nodes from dude on airplane Issue: meshcore does not allow building local mesh networks for hiking and other activities with friends, when you dont have need or/and desire to setup repeater

messages.author: VasiliSk
messages.text: Proposal: 'Last hop(e)' function to expand local coverage and allow creation of small meshes. For large meshes this will allow to connect devices with shitty antennas to clients that have repeater in range. Lost client will choose one of clients as access point to repeater. All comms will go through this client, to minimize broadcast clog. When device gets in range of repeater, switch to normal Client mode. In this scenario Lost Client should be aware of who have repeater access to reach global network. And Client that used as router might need to be aware of Lost. Lost dont rebroadcast messages, only receive in big network. Small mesh: every client in Lost mode. No repeaters around, and with software constraint on visible node count we can activate broadcast for messages, to allow meshtastic-like operation for group of people hiking or riding on bikes in distant areas, without need to configure anything separately. Open app, discover, use. Keep it maximum simple for not advanced users.

No repeaters around, and with software constraint on visible node count we can activate broadcast for messages, to allow meshtastic-like operation for group of people hiking or riding on bikes in distant areas, without need to configure anything separately. Open app, discover, use. Keep it maximum simple for not advanced users.

messages.author: TKW1/Seagull ZL1JFM
messages.text: Most parties would be small - I reckon you are talking 4-6 nodes in the bush - certainly less than a dozen, and should have a frequency away from the main mesh. I think this may be a use case for Meshtastic. If nodes are set up on their own frequency then should work fine.

messages.author: UP
messages.text: Meshtastic is for small networks, which is why Meshcore exists... to allow big networks! Let's don't make Meshcore back into Meshtastic, please. While i totally get the frustration of not being around repeaters, I read through this thread, and still don't see the path to implement this without harming the primary use case of meshcore. I'm still working to understand the guts of the system fully, and am open to being corrected, though. Just remember that part of facilitating a large network is that your available options have to resist the foibles of highly motivated participants whose use case breaks everyone else's. Really, if Meshtastic would also go full flooding instead of RNG managed flooding, then we would have two tools that would each be reliable for their respective use cases... but instead, they also want to keep trying to do everything, rather than focusing in on what they had the potential to actually do well.
messages.content_html: Meshtastic is for small networks, which is why Meshcore exists... to allow big networks! Let's don't make Meshcore back into Meshtastic, please. While i totally get the frustration of not being around repeaters, I read through this thread, and still don't see the path to implement this without harming the primary use case of meshcore. I'm still working to understand the guts of the system fully, and am open to being corrected, though. Just remember that part of facilitating a large network is that your available options have to resist the foibles of highly motivated participants whose use case breaks everyone else's. Really, if Meshtastic would also go full flooding instead of RNG managed flooding, then we would have two tools that would each be reliable for their respective use cases... but instead, they also want to keep trying to do everything, rather than focusing in on what they had the potential to actually do well.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: UP
messages.text: Sometimes, topography requires a lot of repeaters to cover in Meshcore, my region being an excellent example. As I understand it, Meshcore is alternative infrastructure, while Meshtastic is about mobility and not needing infrastructure. The meshcore answer to difficult or remote terrain is more repeaters, which requires dedicating effort to the particular area, but rewards you with scalability and stable, intuitive outcomes.

messages.author: TKW1/Seagull ZL1JFM
messages.text: I think meshcore-sar addresses this need. https://discord.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1430178514476208148 My suggestion for use in an already well established mesh area would be to select a frequency off the regular frequency at a staging point or pre trip planning. Otherwise, in a more sparse area, the regular mesh could enhance the effectiveness. The later is more likely for going bush, while the former might be for traveling in convoy through an assisting mesh. As examples.
messages.content_html: I think meshcore-sar addresses this need. https://discord.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1430178514476208148 My suggestion for use in an already well established mesh area would be to select a frequency off the regular frequency at a staging point or pre trip planning. messages.author: Experimental Cyborg
messages.text: I love this. Simply giving endpoints the ability to mesh together with a TTL of 1 hop. I would like to add a timeout requirement on lost nodes; you can't become lost until you've not seen a repeater AND you have not seen your target endpoint for more than x seconds (i would propose a default of 2 minutes). Receiving endpoints should enforce this as well, meaning they should ignore endpoints that claim to be lost while you've seen them ack a repeater or the endpoint they're asking to be relayed to less than <your lost timeout setting> ago.
messages.content_html: I love this. Simply giving endpoints the ability to mesh together with a TTL of 1 hop. I would like to add a timeout requirement on lost nodes; you can't become lost until you've not seen a repeater AND you have not seen your target endpoint for more than x seconds (i would propose a default of 2 minutes). Receiving endpoints should enforce this as well, meaning they should ignore endpoints that claim to be lost while you've seen them ack a repeater or the endpoint they're asking to be relayed to less than <your lost timeout setting> ago.

messages.author: Purple
messages.text: I think having a relatively arbitrary timeout would be frustrating and confusing to users. But yeah I would agree in general that this is a good feature, maybe when no repeats/ack's are received for a message, that would indicate it has likely been reached centrally, a single attempt to request another client to forward the message could be attempted. The argument against would be that this occupies more airtime (broadcasting twice effectively), but when no repeaters are in reach I'd argue that the frequency is probably not that busy anyway. By making this a default feature, and not a flag someone can set on their client it would reduce the odds of it being used in ways where it negatively affects the network. The feature will do nothing if a single repeater is within range, which would be the case for most if not all airtime busy areas (where would all the airtime otherwise come from right?) My only concern would be that there is some kind of edge case I'm not thinking of that would make this messy and MT like. I also don't know what this would do with the odds of collissions (edited)

I love this. Simply giving endpoints the ability to mesh together with a TTL of 1 hop. I would like to add a timeout requirement on lost nodes; you can't become lost until you've not seen a repeater AND you have not seen your target endpoint for more than x seconds (i would propose a default of 2 minutes). Receiving endpoints should enforce this as well, meaning they should ignore endpoints that claim to be lost while you've seen them ack a repeater or the endpoint they're asking to be relayed to less than <your lost timeout setting> ago.

messages.author: Purple
messages.text: Not sure if I agree, as users may enable this not knowing what it does (hurting the mesh temporarily until it turns off again) or users forget to use this / function expires and get confused why they're not coming through. Maybe as a separate firmware, but that's another thing to maintain at that point. I was thinking of clients automatically becoming repeaters as long as it doesn't see any repeaters within it's own range, but that would also not solve much and just make things messy
